questions.question_text: Which division is equivalent to $0.486\div0.3$ with a whole-number divisor?

options.why: Multiplied only the divisor by $10$, leaving the dividend unchanged.
Multiplied only the dividend by $10$, so the divisor is still a decimal.
Multiplied both by $100$ when $\times10$ already makes the divisor whole.

questions.solution_text:
The divisor $0.3$ has $1$ decimal place, so multiply both numbers by $10$.
$0.486\div0.3=4.86\div3$

questions.question_text: Which division is equivalent to $5.085\div0.03$ with a whole-number divisor?

options.why: Multiplied both by only $10$, so the divisor $0.3$ is still a decimal.
Multiplied both by $1000$, over-shifting by one power of $10$.
Multiplied the dividend by $100$ but the divisor by only $10$.

questions.solution_text:
The divisor $0.03$ has $2$ decimal places, so multiply both numbers by $100$.
$5.085\div0.03=508.5\div3$

questions.question_text: Which division is equivalent to $4.5\div0.002$ with a whole-number divisor?

options.why: Multiplied both by only $100$, leaving the divisor $0.2$ a decimal.
Multiplied both by $10000$, over-shifting by one power of $10$.
Multiplied the dividend by $1000$ but the divisor by only $100$.

questions.solution_text:
The divisor $0.002$ has $3$ decimal places, so multiply both numbers by $1000$.
$4.5\div0.002=4500\div2$

questions.question_text: Which division is equivalent to $17\div0.4$ with a whole-number divisor?

options.why: Made the divisor whole but forgot to multiply the dividend $17$ by $10$ too.
Multiplied the dividend by $10$ but left the divisor unchanged.
Multiplied both by $100$ when the divisor has only $1$ decimal place.

questions.solution_text:
The divisor $0.4$ has $1$ decimal place, so multiply both numbers by $10$.
The whole-number dividend $17$ gains a zero.
$17\div0.4=170\div4$
